Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to block out time on weekends since I work during the week, but it rarely went according to plan. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend time creating diagrams and color-coded notes, but finding consistent hours was a struggle. With five online courses running simultaneously, I often felt like I was just trying to keep my head above water rather than following any structured routine.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. I'd have good intentions to study at regular times, but then anxiety would spike and I'd either freeze up or hyperfocus right before something was due. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything. The inconsistency frustrated me because I know I'm capable of more, but my energy levels and mental state just wouldn't cooperate.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification that something new had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my anxiety made me paranoid about falling behind. I also logged in frequently to watch the video lectures since those were my primary way of absorbing the material. The fear of missing something often drove me more than genuine curiosity, which I'm not proud of.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic, like checking email. I'd scroll through announcements and see what was coming up. But that routine fell apart whenever my stress levels got too high—I'd start avoiding it because opening the platform made me anxious. The mindfulness exercises I was doing helped a bit, but building consistent habits has been really hard for me this semester.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched over 90 minutes worth because that's how I learn best. Being able to pause, rewind, and see concepts visually explained made a huge difference. I also found the peer interactions helpful when I actually engaged with them; discussing ideas with classmates helped things click. The visual materials like diagrams and infographics stuck with me way better than walls of text.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials and some of the discussion forums when they felt like busywork. If something was text-heavy without visual elements, I'd skim it or ignore it entirely. Some supplementary resources just felt overwhelming given everything else I had to do. I know I probably missed valuable content, but I had to triage based on what my brain could actually process.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to the assessments or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. Visual content always felt more worthwhile because I could engage with it more easily. If something felt like it was just checking a box without actually teaching me anything, I'd lose interest quickly. The peer discussions were hit or miss—sometimes incredibly valuable, sometimes just noise.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With my completion rate already suffering and my anxiety about falling behind, I focused almost exclusively on required work. There was always this guilt about not doing the optional stuff, but I had to protect my mental energy. Required activities came with deadlines and consequences, which honestly was the only thing that got me moving some weeks.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded lecture slides and would study them on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I made extensive handwritten notes with diagrams and mind maps because that's how information sticks for me. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp from the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen entirely.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be reviewing my printed notes or redrawing diagrams to memorize processes, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours with a whiteboard working through problems, talking myself through concepts out loud. Sometimes I'd discuss course material with a friend over coffee, working through difficult ideas together. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study time.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd watch a video once online to get the gist, then download the slides and work with them offline for days. My internet is reliable, but I found I focused better when I wasn't connected and couldn't get distracted. I'd take screenshots of important diagrams and organize them in folders on my tablet for repeated review. The initial access was just the starting point of my engagement with that material.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal—my anxiety peaked and I found myself paralyzed, unable to even open the LMS some days. I had competing responsibilities at work and my low resilience meant I crashed hard when things piled up. There were weeks where I'd stare at my to-do list and feel so overwhelmed that I'd do nothing instead. The mindfulness intervention helped me recognize these patterns, but breaking out of them was still incredibly difficult.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in but just passively scroll without actually engaging. Confusion about assignment requirements sometimes made me avoid starting them entirely until the last minute. My other courses competed for attention constantly, and some weeks certain classes just got neglected. The fatigue was real—by evening, my brain was just done, even if I had time to study.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely, or I'd click through readings without absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd log in and just sit there, feeling too anxious to actually do anything productive. Those sessions looked like engagement in the data but were essentially me going through the motions while my brain checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew exactly how important they were. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like there was a wall between me and the task. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same or worse. It's a frustrating cycle because I'm aware of what I'm doing but feel powerless to stop it. The bigger or more complex the task, the more I'd avoid it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have these intense bursts of activity in the 24-48 hours before something was due. My average response time of over 21 hours reflects that last-minute scramble pattern. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have completed anything because the structure they provided was the only thing cutting through my avoidance. It wasn't healthy, but it was functional.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a brief boost, but criticism, even constructive criticism, would send my anxiety spiraling. Sometimes I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get helpful feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar content, but the emotional toll of potentially negative feedback often outweighed that benefit.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. I appreciated being able to work at my own pace, but without rigid structure, my anxiety-driven avoidance had room to flourish. The modules were well-organized visually, which helped my learning style. Having five courses simultaneously made it hard to give any single course the attention its structure deserved.
Q19: The visual elements and video content made engagement easier—those aligned with how I learn. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or buried in text-heavy documents. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made me engage superficially rather than meaningfully. Having clear, visual roadmaps of what was expected and when would have helped me manage my anxiety better.
Q20: I think that pattern describes me somewhat—I logged in frequently and watched lots of videos but my assignment completion was only around 54%. For me, it was anxiety blocking the final step of actually submitting work. You can be genuinely trying to learn and engage with material but still struggle to produce tangible outputs. Sometimes high activity is someone desperately trying to understand but not quite getting there.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. If someone learns efficiently through methods that aren't tracked—like I do with my offline visual studying—their LMS activity wouldn't reflect their actual preparation. Some people just need less time with materials to understand them. I think my exam score being decent despite my attendance issues shows that my offline studying was actually effective, even if the platform couldn't see it.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like login frequency, time spent on videos, and submission timing. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone is actually absorbing material or just going through motions. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the genuine understanding happening in someone's mind. The numbers tell a story, but it's missing crucial chapters.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious and making connections between concepts, creating those visual maps in my head. True engagement is when I'm thinking about course content even when I'm not actively studying. It's not about time logged or boxes checked—it's about whether the learning is actually happening inside me.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels or how hard I fought just to open the platform some days. It doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials and hand-drawn diagrams, or the peer discussions that happened outside the platform. It can't measure the internal struggle between wanting to succeed and feeling paralyzed by fear of failure. My engagement was real, even when the data made it look inconsistent.
